channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: Gravity as Geometry
item.description:
Newton said that masses attract each other with a force proportional to their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. This works extraordinarily well for most purposes. It sent Apollo to the Moon and Voyager past Neptune. It is also wrong – or rather, it is an approximation of something deeper.

The elevator thought experiment

Einstein began with a simple observation: if you are in a sealed elevator in free fall, you cannot tell whether you are falling in a gravitational field or floating in empty space. Gravity and acceleration are locally indistinguishable. This is the equivalence principle, and it was Einstein’s starting point for general relativity.

item.title: Open Questions in Cosmology
item.description:
The Lambda-CDM model fits the data. It does not answer the questions. Here are the five deepest open problems in cosmology, each representing a gap in our understanding of fundamental physics.

1. What is dark matter?

Dark matter constitutes about 27% of the universe’s energy content. Its gravitational effects are observed at every scale from individual galaxies to the cosmic microwave background. But its particle identity is unknown.

Leading candidates include weakly interacting massive particles (WIMPs), axions, and sterile neutrinos. Direct detection experiments (LUX, XENON, PandaX) have progressively excluded large regions of the WIMP parameter space without a detection. Indirect searches (gamma rays from dark matter annihilation) and collider searches (production at the LHC) have also yielded null results.

item.title: The Lambda-CDM Model
item.description:
Modern cosmology has a standard model, and it is embarrassingly successful.

What the model says

The Lambda-CDM model describes a universe that:

  • Began in a hot, dense state approximately 13.8 billion years ago (the Big Bang)
  • Has been expanding ever since, with the expansion now accelerating
  • Is spatially flat (the spatial geometry is Euclidean)
  • Contains roughly 5% baryonic matter, 27% cold dark matter, and 68% dark energy (the cosmological constant Λ\Lambda)
  • Satisfies the cosmological principle: homogeneous and isotropic on large scales

The dynamics are governed by the Friedmann equations, which follow from general relativity applied to a homogeneous, isotropic universe. The model has six free parameters. Given these six numbers, it predicts:

item.title: What Is Cosmology?
item.description:
You have looked at the night sky and seen stars. Cosmology asks a different question than astronomy: not “what is that star?” but “why is there a sky at all?”

Astronomy studies individual objects – stars, planets, nebulae. Astrophysics studies the physical processes inside those objects – how stars burn, how planets form, how supernovae explode. Cosmology studies the universe as a single physical system. It asks questions that cannot be answered by examining any individual object, no matter how carefully:

item.title: Why Spacetime Is One Thing
item.description:
You already have an intuition for space – it is where things are. And you have an intuition for time – it is when things happen. The discovery that space and time are not independent but are aspects of a single entity is one of the most important results in physics.

The problem with light

In the late 19th century, physicists assumed light traveled through a medium (the “luminiferous ether”) the way sound travels through air. If so, the speed of light should depend on the observer’s motion through the ether. In 1887, Michelson and Morley tested this with an interferometer. The result: the speed of light is the same regardless of the observer’s motion. This was baffling.
